solutions.solution:
Statements: G = K, F > I, K ≥ Q, Q ≥ F
Conclusions: I. K = F
II. F < K
From K ≥ Q and Q ≥ F we get K ≥ Q ≥ F so F ≤ K ≥ F

Statements: I. T > K, S ≤ Z, U ≥ W, S = R
Conclusions: I. U > K
Combining U ≥ W and W > S we get U > W > S which gives U > S
S = K gives U > K. So, I follows. From, K < Z and S = K we get S < Z which is different from Z > S.

Statements: G = E, D < K, E < S, K ≤ G
Conclusions: I. S > D
II.D < E
From, G = E and K < G we have K ≤ E. With K ≤ E and D < K, we get D < K ≤ E to get D < E. So, II follows. Now, D < E and E < S gives D < S. So, I follows.

Statements: W ≥ K, S ≤ Z, X ≥ W, S = K
Conclusions: I. K < Z
II. X > K
I is not true as there are no equalities with Z in the given statement (Tip 7: quick method). II follows from X ≥ W and W ≥ K.

Statements: L ≥ R, N = M, L < M, N ≤ P
Conclusions: I. N ≤ R
II. R ≤ P
From L < M and L ≥ R; It follows. II must be wrong as P is not smaller than any quantity in the given statements. (Tip 8(i): quick method).

Statements: P < Q, W ≥ N, P ≥ N, T < N
Conclusions: I. P ≥ W
II. Q < W
I must be wrong as there are no equalities in the given statements (Tip 7: quick method). II must be wrong as Q is not less than any number in the given statements. (Tip 8(i): quick method).

Statements: D ≤ S, N > V, D = R, R < V
Conclusions: I. S ≥ V
II.D > N
Combining D ≤ S and D = R we get R ≤ S. Now, combining R < S and R > N we get V < S to get V < S. II must be wrong because (Tip 8(i): quick method) D is not greater than anything in the given statement.

Statements: I. K = H, K ≥ M, E < M
Conclusions: I. M > P
II. M = P
From K = P and K ≥ M we have P ≤ M. By check 3, either I or II follows.

Statements: T < G, L < T, C = L, S > C
Conclusions: I. G > L
II. L < S
From, T ≤ G and L < T; I follows. II follows from C = L and S > C.

Statements: J > P, S ≥ J, A = S, K < A
Conclusions: I. A > J
II. A > J
From A = S and S ≥ J; we have A ≥ J. By check 3, either I or II follows.
